LAW AND MOTION 302, DEFENDANT BANK OF THE WEST'S MOTION TO QUASH NOTICE OF DEPOSITION OF MICHAEL BRACCO, SENIOR EXECUTIVE VICE PRESIDENT AND CHIEF ADMINISTRATIVE OFFICER FOR BANK OF THE WEST, FOR PROTECTIVE ORDER RE SAME, AND FOR SANCTIONS IN THE AMOUNT OF $2,500 IS DENIED. A PLAINTIFF NEED NOT EXHAUST OTHER METHODS OF DISCOVERY WHEN THE APEX INDIVIDUAL HAS UNIQUE OR SUPERIOR PERSONAL KNOWLEDGE OF DISCOVERABLE INFORMATION. (LIBERTY MUTUAL INS. CO. V. SUPERIOR COURT (1992) 10 CAL. APP. 4TH 1282, 1289.) , THE UNDISPUTED EVIDENCE IS THAT PLAINTIFF SENT AN EMAIL TO BANK OF THE WEST'S CEO, MICHAEL SHEPHERD (WHO SHE HAD PREVIOUSLY MET) COMPLAINING THAT SHE WAS RESIGNING FROM THE BANK BECAUSE "[F]OR SEVERAL MONTHS I HAD TO ENDURE A BARRAGE OF INSULTS AND SEXUAL HARASSMENT FROM NOLEN BOYER . . . ." MR. SHEPHERD FORWARDED HER EMAIL TO MICHAEL BRACCO WITH INSTRUCTIONS TO "PLEASE LOOK INTO THESE COMMENTS . . . . IF YOU FIND THEY ARE SUBSTANTIATED, PLEASE BRIEF ME AND MAKE ANY RECOMMENDATIONS YOU BELIEVE APPROPRIATE." (STEPHENSON DECL. IN OPPOSITION TO MOTION TO QUASH, EXHIBIT K). MR. BRACCO HAS UNIQUE OR SUPERIOR PERSONAL KNOWLEDGE OF DISCOVERABLE INFORMATION, AND PLAINTIFF IS ENTITLED TO TAKE MR. BRACCO'S DEPOSITION. ORDER SIGNED IN OPEN COURT. JUDGE: A. JAMES ROBERTSON II; CLERK: LESLEY FISCELLA, COURT REPORTER: LAURA MARTINEZ, CSR #11332

LAW AND MOTION, 302, RULING - A R G U E D; THE COURT ADOPTED ITS TENTATIVE RULING. PLAINTIFF NETSANET HAILEMICHAEL'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION IS DENIED WITHOUT PREJUDICE. PLAINTIFF HAS NOT MET ITS INITIAL BURDEN. PLAINTIFF RELIES ON DEFENDANT'S INSUFFICIENT RESPONSES TO ITS SPECIAL INTERROGATORIES AS THE PRIMARY BASIS FOR THIS MOTION. HOWEVER, ON JANUARY 18, 2014, THE DAY AFTER PLAINTIFF FILED THIS MOTION, DEFENDANT SERVED AMENDED RESPONSES TO PLAINTIFF'S SPECIAL INTERROGATORIES PURSUANT TO AN ORDER OF THIS COURT GRANTING PLAINTIFF'S MOTION TO COMPEL. BECAUSE THE RESPONSES PLAINTIFF RELIES ON HAVE BEEN SUPERSEDED BY AMENDED RESPONSES, THIS MOTION IS DENIED WITHOUT PREJUDICE. ORDER SIGNED IN OPEN COURT. JUDGE: ERNEST H. GOLDSMITH, CLERK: CYNTHIA HERBERT; COURT REPORTER: SHERRY SAWYER, CSR # 5976

RULING - A R G U E D The Court adopted its tentative ruling. DEFENDANTS SINOPA GROUP LLC and NOLEN BOYER's MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT is Denied. In 1959, the legislature passed the Unruh Act to expand "the reach of [the prior public accommodations statute] from common carriers and places of public accommodation and recreation'to include 'all business establishments of every kind whatsoever.'" (Marina Point, Ltd. v. Wolfson (1982) 30 Cal.3d 721, 731.) "Any business or organization, whether for-profit, or non-profit, may be deemed a business establishment if it performs customary business functions and fulfills a businesslike purpose." (O'Connor v. Village Green Owners Assn. (1983) 33 Cal.3d 790, 796.) The evidence establishes that Defendants are a business that provides project management, cost management, and development consulting to business clients. (UMF no. 2.) As such, Defendants are a "business establishment[] of every kind whatsoever" covered by the Unruh Act. (See Wilkins-Jones v. County of Alameda (N.D. Cal. 2012) 859 F.Supp.2d 1039, applying California law, holding that a for-profit, private contractor hired by a county to provide medical care assessment services for the county jail inmates was a business establishment as contemplated by the Unruh Act.) California cases have "allowed parties that are not clients, patrons, or customers in the traditional sense to bring Unruh Act claims." (Strother v. Southern California Permanente Medical Group (9th Cir. 1996) 79 F.3d 859, 873.) In Jackson v. Sup. Ct. (1994) 30 Cal.App.4th 936, 942, the court held that an investment advisor who accompanied two clients to a bank could bring an Unruh Act claim against the bank because he was "pursuing an accommodation, advantage, or privilege of the bank within the meaning of the Unruh Act when he accompanied his clients to the bank to aid them in transacting their business." The evidence establishes here that Plaintiff was aiding her employer, Bank of the West, in transacting its business with Defendants when the alleged discrimination occurred. Therefore, the conduct complained of falls under the Unruh Act. Lastly, Plaintiff was not an employee or independent contractor of Defendants and thus the Unruh Act applies. Plaintiff was not compensated for her work by Defendants. Rather, Defendants were hired and paid to provide services to Plaintiff's employer, and by virtue of that employment relationship, to Plaintiff. (See Alch v. Sup. Ct. (2004) 122 Cal.App.4th 339, 393, fn. 56.) ORDER SIGNED IN OPEN COURT. Judge: Ernest H. Goldsmith, Clerk: Cynthia Herbert ; Court Reporter: Sherry Sawyer, CSR # 5976 =(302/EHG)

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or In The Alternative For Summary Adjudication
Off Calendar Apr-17-2014 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: APPEARANCES - ATTY. MAT STEPHENSON & ELENA JACOBS OF KUCHAN & STEPHENSON, TEL. NO. 649-1130 APPEARING FOR THE PLAINTIFF;
ATTY. NATALIE PIERCE OF THE LITTLER MENDELSON, P.C., TEL. NO. 288-6321 APPEARING FOR THE DEFENDANT BANK OF THE WEST.

RULING - A R G U E D; DEFENDANT BANK OF THE WEST's MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or In The Alternative For Summary Adjudication- Defendant's motion for summary judgment is denied. Defendant's motion for summary adjudication is granted in part and denied in part. Issues 1-11 set forth in the Notice are subsumed in the ruling on each cause of action. As to the first cause of action, summary adjudication is denied. Plaintiff has raised triable issues as to whether Boyer had discriminatory intent, whether the conduct was severe or pervasive enough to give rise to harassment, whether Defendant took immediate and appropriate corrective action, and whether the alleged discriminatory conduct was taken within the course and scope of agency or employment. As to the second cause of action, summary adjudication is granted. "[T]o establish constructive discharge, an employee must plead and prove'that the employer either intentionally created or knowingly permitted working conditions that were so intolerable or aggravated at the time of the employee's resignation that a reasonable employer would realize that a reasonable person in the employee's position would be compelled to resign." (Turner v. Anheuser-Busch, Inc. (1994) 7 Cal.4th 1238, 1251.) The evidence does not raise a triable issue of material fact as to whether Plaintiff had "no reasonable alternative except to quit." (Id.) The undisputed facts show that Defendant informed Plaintiff prior to her resignation that Plaintiff would never work on a project with Boyer again (AMF 65), Defendant informed Plaintiff prior to her resignation that Boyer would not work for Defendant in the future (UMF 52), Plaintiff informed her friend in Florida prior to her resignation that moving there was her "dream" (UMF 36, 76), Plaintiff desired to move to Florida to "recover [from her] financial situation" and to "enjoy life" by getting rid of her mortgage so she would have "free money on hand so [she] can live [her] life" (UMF 41, 45), and Plaintiff offered to continue working for Defendant at the time of her resignation, provided that she could work remotely from Florida (AMF 71). As to the third cause of action, summary adjudication is granted as unopposed. As to the fourth cause of action, summary adjudication is granted. The FEHC has explicitly held that Gov. Code sec. 12940(k) does not "give rise to an actionable tort or private cause of action in civil proceedings between private litigants." (DFEH v. Lyddan Law Group (Cal. F.E.H.C. 2010) 2010 CAFEHC Lexis 4, *35.) Courts "ordinarily give substantial weight to the FEHC's construction of the statutes under which it operates" unless "the agency's interpretation is clearly erroneous or unauthorized." (Kelly v. Methodist Hospital of Southern Cal. (2000) 22 Cal.4th 1108, 1118.) As to the twelfth issue, summary adjudication is denied. Defendant is seeking to adjudicate that damages "will be limited." (Notice, p. 2.) This issue cannot be properly disposed of by summary adjudication. (CCP sec. 437c(f)(1).) As to the thirteenth issue, summary adjudication is granted. The undisputed facts show that Plaintiff offered to continue work, so long as she could do so remotely in Florida. (AMF 71.) As to the fourteenth issue, summary adjudication is denied. There is a triable issue as to whether Thomason was informed by other employees about Boyer's treatment of Plaintiff, but did not take immediate remedial action. (AMF 73-75.). ORDER SIGNED IN OPEN COURT. Judge: Ernest H. Goldsmith, Clerk: Cynthia Herbert ; Court Reporter: Sherry Sawyer, CSR # 5976 =(302/EHG)

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Quash Notice Of Deposition Of Michael Bracco, Senior Executive Vice President And Chief Administrative Officer For Bank Of The West, For Protective Order Re Same, And For Sanctions In The Amount Of $2,500
Off Calendar Dec-16-2013 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances: MATTHEW STEPHENSON, ESQ., 510.649.1130, FOR PLAINTIFF NETSANET HAILEMICHAEL.
AURELIO PEREZ, ESQ., 415.288.6394, FOR DEFENDANT BANK OF THE WEST.

RULING: Argued and the Court adopted its tentative ruling: DEFENDANT BANK OF THE WEST'S Motion To Quash Notice Of Deposition Of Michael Bracco, Senior Executive Vice President And Chief Administrative Officer For Bank Of The West, For Protective Order Re Same, And For Sanctions In The Amount Of $2,500 IS Denied. A plaintiff need not exhaust other methods of discovery when the apex individual has unique or superior personal knowledge of discoverable information. (Liberty Mutual Ins. Co. v. Superior Court (1992) 10 Cal. App. 4th 1282, 1289.) , The undisputed evidence is that Plaintiff sent an email to Bank of the West's CEO, Michael Shepherd (who she had previously met) complaining that she was resigning from the bank because "[f]or several months I had to endure a barrage of insults and sexual harassment from Nolen Boyer . . . ." Mr. Shepherd forwarded her email to Michael Bracco with instructions to "Please look into these comments . . . . If you find they are substantiated, please brief me and make any recommendations you believe appropriate." (Stephenson Decl. in Opposition to Motion to Quash, Exhibit K). Mr. Bracco has unique or superior personal knowledge of discoverable information, and Plaintiff is entitled to take Mr. Bracco's deposition. Order signed in open court. Judge: A. James Robertson II; Clerk: Lesley Fiscella, Court Reporter: Laura Martinez, CSR #11332

Ntc Of Mtn And Mtn To Quash Deft Bank Of The West' Subpoenas And Request For A Monetary Sanction In The Amount Of $2415 Against Deft And Its Counsel Littler Mendelson Pc
Off Calendar Nov-25-2013 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES - ATTY. MATHEW STEPHENSON OF KOCHAN & STEPHENSON, TEL. NO. (510) 649-1130 APPEARING FOR THE PLAINTIFF;
ATTY. AURELIO PEREZ OF THE LITTLER LAW FIRM, TEL. NO. 288-6394 APPEARING FOR THE DEFENDANT.

RULING - A R G U E D; THE COURT MODIFIED ITS TENTATIVE RULING AND RULED AS FOLLOWS: PLAINTIFF NETSANET HAILEMICHAEL's Motion To Quash Defendant Bank Of The West' Subpoenas And Request For A Monetary Sanction In The Amount Of $2415 Against Defendant And Its Counsel Littler Mendelson Pc - Motion to Quash is Granted, in part as the subpoenas for medical and dental records. The description of the DOCUMENTS sought will be modified to read as follows: (1) any preexisting ulcerous condition of NETSANET HAILEMICHAEL, or (2) any clinical depression of NETSANET HAILEMICHAEL, or (3) any Documents that describe any medical condition, course of treatment, or medication that contributed to or impacted a pre-existing ulcerous condition or clinical depression of NETSANET HAILEMICHAEL is Granted in part and denied in part as to the subpoena for the employment records of Bank of America. The only records for which the defendant's interests overcome the privacy objections concern the last year of employment and any "for cause" grounds for termination of employment. Accordingly, the motion is granted as to all employment records that pre-date the final year of employment, and the motion is denied as to the limited request for records during the last year of employment or that concern the grounds for termination of employment in the last year. Assuming that the date of termination was May 29, 2002, the description of the records sought is modified to include only those records for the last year of employment and would read as follows: "Any and all records from May 29, 2001 and all records related to NETSANET HAILEMICHAEL's termination of employment in 2002."
Granted as to the motion to quash the subpoena on the Custodian of Records for Bank of the West for the short sale documents. Defendants failed to demonstrate a nexus between any financial disclosures made in connection with a short sale application and the damages sought. Defendants already have a copy of the short sale application from the plaintiff.
As to the claim for set-off for forgiven debt, defendants offers no legal basis for such a defense. Finally, even assuming such an offset could be lawfully applied, the information for the amount of the offset is in the possession of the bank and does not depend upon production of the consumer's records to be ascertained. The request for sanctions is granted in the amount of $1,500. ORDER SIGNED IN OPEN COURT. Judge: Ernest H. Goldsmith, Clerk: Cynthia Herbert; Court Reporter: Anthony C. Vaughn, CSR # 6185 =302/ehg

Notice Of Motion To Compel Deft'S Further Responses To Form Interrogatories - Employment Law, Special Interrogatories And Award Of Monetary Sanction Against Deft And Its Counsel Littler Mendelson In The Amouht Of $2,665
Off Calendar Nov-07-2013 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES - ATTY. DEBORAH KOCHAN, TEL. NO. (510) 649-1130 APPEARING FOR THE PLAINTIFF;
ATTY. AURELIO PEREZ OF LITTLER LAW FIRM, TEL. NO. 288-6394 APPEARING FOR THE DEFENDANT;
TAMARA WILLAT OF WILLAT COURT REPORTING, TEL. NO. 272-1753, COURT REPORTER.

RULING - A R G U E D; The Court adopted its tentative ruling. PLAINTIFF NETSANET HAILEMICHAEL Notice Of Motion To Compel Deft'S Further Responses To Form Interrogatories - Employment Law, Special Interrogatories And Award Of Monetary Sanction Against Deft And Its Counsel Littler Mendelson In The Amouht Of $2,665 - Defendant's general objections and specific objections violated CCP 2030.300(a)(3) because they are too general and are sanctionable "boilerplate" per Korea Data Systems Co. Ltd. v. Sup. Ct. (Amazing Technologies Corp.) (1997) 51 CA4th, 1513, 1516.
Defendant shall answer Special Interrogatories 1-6, inclusive, without objection within 15 days of notice of entry of this order.
With respect to contact information requested in Form Interrogatories (Employment) 207.2 and 217.1, the motion is Denied. The witnesses are current or former officers, managers, or employees who are percipient witnesses and whose "act[s] or failure to act in connection with the matter . . . may bind the corporation, be imputed to it, or constitute an admission of the corporation for purposes of establishing liability." (Triple A Machine Shop, Inc. v. State of California (1989) 213 Cal.App.3d 131, 140.) Accordingly, they may not be contacted by plaintiff's counsel independently by virtue of RPC 2-100(B)(2). plaintiff can take their depositions upon notice to defendant's counsel. Hence, their privacy rights outweigh plaintiff's interest in their contact information. Life Technologies Corp. v. Superior Court (Timothy H. Joyce) (2011) 197 Cal.App.4th 640, 655. If plaintiff does not produce former employees or officers for deposition upon notice, then plaintiff is entitled to their contact information, subject to an appropriately drafted confidentiality protective order or agreement. Motion for sanctions is Denied. No sanctions are awarded to either party.
(Prevailing party to prepare a form of order and to comply with CRC 3.1312) Judge: Ernest H. Goldsmith, Clerk: Cynthia Herbert; Court Reporter: Tamara Willat, CSR # 4609 =302/EHG
